elements.sent-2411-001: The monotone path polytope of a polytope $\polytopeP$ encapsulates the combinatorial behavior of the shadow vertex rule (a pivot rule used in linear programming) on $\polytopeP$.
elements.sent-2411-002: On the other hand, computing monotone path polytopes is the entry door to the larger subject of fiber polytopes, for which explicitly computing examples remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-003: Monotone path polytopes of cubes and simplices have been known since the seminal article of Billera and Strumfels \cite{BilleraSturmfels-FiberPolytope .}
elements.sent-2411-004: We (partially) extend these results to hypersimplices by linking this problem to the combinatorics of lattice paths.
elements.sent-2411-005: Indeed, we give a combinatorial model to describe the vertices of the monotone path polytope of the hypersimplex $\HypSimplTwo$ (for any generic direction).
elements.sent-2411-006: With this model, we give a precise count of these vertices, and furthermore count the number of coherent monotone paths on $\HypSimplTwo$ according to their lengths.
elements.sent-2411-007: We prove that some of the results obtained also hold for hypersimplices $\HypSimpl$ for $k\geq 2$.

elements.sent-2411-011: After their introduction by Billera and Sturmfels in \cite{BilleraSturmfels-FiberPolytope , fiber polytopes have received a lot of attention.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In particular, the fiber polytope for the projection of a polytope $\polytopeP$ onto a segment encapsulates the combinatorics of (coherent) monotone paths on $\polytopeP$.
elements.sent-2411-013: For this reason, it is called the \defn{monotone path polytope of $\polytopeP$ \cite{athanasiadis1999piles,athanasiadis2000monotone,blanchard2020length .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The vertices of a monotone path polytope are in bijection with the monotone paths that can be followed by a shadow vertex rule.
elements.sent-2411-015: As such, it links the world of linear optimization to the world of triangulations.
elements.sent-2411-016: This, and the fact that monotone path polytopes stand among the easiest fiber polytopes to compute, have motivated numerous studies on the subject.
elements.sent-2411-017: Especially, the monotone path polytope of a simplex is a cube \cite{BilleraSturmfels-FiberPolytope , the one of a cube is a permutahedron \cite[Example 9.8]{BilleraSturmfels-FiberPolytope,Ziegler-polytopes , the one of a cyclic polytope is a cyclic zonotope \cite{Athanasiadis_2000 , the one of a cross-polytope is the signohedron \cite{BlackDeLoera2021monotone , and the one of an $S$-hypersimplex is a permutahedron \cite{ManeckeSanyalSo2019shypersimplices .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, monotone path polytopes of the hypersimplices have not yet been fully explored.

elements.subsection-2411-001: Explicit construction(s) of monotone path polytopes
elements.sent-2411-028: In general, fiber polytopes are, by construction, complicated to compute.
elements.sent-2411-029: As a simple case, fiber polytopes for projections onto a point are trivial, as the fiber polytope of a polytope $\polytopeP$ onto a point equals $\polytopeP$ itself.
elements.sent-2411-030: Hence, among the first cases one would want to investigate are the fiber polytopes associated to projections onto a 1-dimensional polytope, \ie a segment.
elements.sent-2411-031: We state here the formal definition coming from fiber polytopes for reference, but we will never use it, preferring instead the reformulation of \Cref{thm:MPPSumSectionAtVertices,thm:FaceLatticeMPP : the non-expert reader does not need the following definition to understand the results thereafter.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In this article, a (bounded) \defn{linear program $(\polytopeP, \b c)$} is a couple formed by a polytope $\polytopeP\subset \R^d$ and a vector $\b c\in \R^d$ that shall be thought as the direction along which we want to optimize.
elements.sent-2411-033: Except if we say otherwise, we always take a \defn{generic direction $\b c$, that is to say $\inner{\b c, \b v - \b u \ne 0$ for every edge $\b u\b v$ of $\polytopeP$.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: This ensures that the scalar product against $\b c$ is minimized (respectively maximized) by a unique vertex of $\polytopeP$: \defn{$\b v_{\min := \text{argmin}\{\inner{\b c, \b x ~;~\b x\in \polytopeP\}$} and \defn{$\b v_{\max := \text{argmax}\{\inner{\b c, \b x ~;~\b x\in \polytopeP\}$}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: For a linear program $(\polytopeP,\b c)$, the \defn{monotone path polytope $\MPP$ is the fiber polytope for the projection $\pi_{\b c :\b x\mapsto\inner{\b x,\b c}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Denoting the image segment $\polytopeQ = \pi_{\b c (\polytopeP) = \{\inner{\b x,\b c ~;~ \b x\in \polytopeP\}$, one has: \begin{equation}\MPP := \left\{\int_{\polytopeQ} \gamma(x)dx ~~;~~ \gamma  section of  \pi_{\b c}\right\}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The polytope $\MPP$ has dimension $\dim(\polytopeP)-1$ but is embedded in $\R^{\dim(\polytopeP)}$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The monotone path polytope, though arising from a fiber polytope point of view, is deeply linked to linear programming.
elements.sent-2411-039: The following theorem formalizes this link.
elements.sent-2411-040: We will then detail some examples, in order to clarify the definitions.
elements.sent-2411-041: For a linear program $(\polytopeP,\b c)$, a \defn{cellular string is a sequence $\sigma = (\polytopeF_1,\dots,\polytopeF_k)$ of faces of $\polytopeP$ (of dimensions at least 1) such that $\min(\polytopeF_1) = \b v_{\min}$, $\max(\polytopeF_k) = \b v_{\max}$, and for all $i\in [k-1]$, $\max(\polytopeF_i) = \min(\polytopeF_{i+1 )$, where minima and maxima are taken with respect to the scalar product against $\b c$.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Cellular strings are ordered by containment of their union to form a partially ordered set (which is known to be a lattice).
elements.sent-2411-043: The \defn{finest cellular strings are the smallest (non-empty) cellular strings for this order, \ie sequences of edges.}
elements.sent-2411-044: For a linear program $(\polytopeP, \b c)$ and a secondary direction $\b\omega\in \R^d$ (linearly independent to $\b c$), one can consider the polygon \defn{$\polytopeP_{\b c,\b \omega}$} obtained by projecting $\polytopeP$ onto the plane spanned by $(\b c, \b \omega)$: \begin{equation}\polytopeP_{\b c, \b \omega} := \left\{\bigl(\inner{\b x,\b c},~\inner{\b x,\b \omega}\bigr) ~;~ \b x\in \polytopeP\right\}\end{equation} A proper face (vertex or edge) $\polytope{G}$ of $\polytopeP_{\b c, \b \omega}$ is an \defn{upper face if it has an outer normal vector with positive second coordinate\footnote{Some definitions in the literature use lower faces, we take upper faces to ease drawings and notations.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: A cellular string $\sigma$ is \defn{coherent if there exists $\b\omega\in \R^d$ such that $\sigma$ is the family of pre-images by~$\pi_{\b c}$ of the lower faces of $\polytopeP_{\b c, \b \omega}$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: In this case, such an $\b\omega$ is said to \defn{capture the cellular string $\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The face lattice of $\MPP$ is the lattice of coherent cellular strings on $\polytopeP$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Fix a polytope $\polytopeP\subset\R^d$, and a generic direction $\b c\in \R^d$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Each edge $\b u\b v$ of $\polytopeP$ is oriented by $\b c$, from $\b u$ to $\b v$ if and only if $\inner{\b c, \b u < \inner{\b c, \b v}$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The finest cellular strings are \defn{monotone paths on $\polytopeP$, that is to say a sequence of vertices $\b v_0, \b v_1, \dots, \b v_r$ such that $\b v_0 = \b v_{\min}$, $\b v_r = \b v_{\max}$ and $\b v_i\b v_{i+1}$ is an edge of $\polytopeP$ directed by $\b c$ from $\b v_i$ to $\b v_{i+1}$.}

elements.paragraph-2411-001: Exploring the space of $\b\omega$, and projection of the normal of $\polytopeP$
elements.sent-2411-061: First of all, \Cref{def:CoherentCellularString invites us to focus on the space of all $\b \omega$, and partition it depending on the coherent cellular strings they yield.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Precisely, to a cellular string $\sigma$ we associate $\c N(\sigma) = \{\b\omega~;~\b\omega \text{ captures \sigma\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Then $\c N(\sigma)$ is a polyhedral cone by linearity (in $\b \omega$) of the projection from $\polytopeP$ onto $\polytopeP_{\b c, \b \omega}$, and the family $\c N = \bigl(\c N(\sigma)\bigr)_{\sigma}$ is a fan.
elements.sent-2411-064: This fan is exactly the normal fan of $\MPP$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Hence, one can run through all possible $\b\omega\in\R^d$, orthogonal to $\b c$ (as all $\b\omega + \lambda\b c$ capture the same cellular string for any $\lambda\in \R$), to draw the normal fan of $\MPP$, see \Cref{fig:AnimatedMPPSimplex for the construction.}
elements.sent-2411-066: This construction shows an important property: for a fixed $\b\omega$, all $\b \omega+\lambda\b c$ for $\lambda\in \R$ capture the same cellular string.
elements.sent-2411-067: Consequently, one can obtain the normal fan of $\MPP$ by projecting the normal fan of $\polytopeP$: to each normal cone $\polytope{C}\in \c N_{\polytopeP}$, associate its projection along $\b c$, namely $\polytope{C _{\perp := \{\b x - \frac{\inner{\b x,\b c {\inner{\b c,\b c }\b c ; \b x\in \polytope{C}\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The common refinement of $\bigl(\polytope{C _{\perp ; \polytope{C}\in\c N_{\polytopeP}\bigr)$ is the normal fan\footnote{This construction embeds the fan $\c N_{\MPP}$ directly into the hyperplane $\b c^{\perp}$, instead of embedding it in $\R^{\dim(\polytopeP)}$.. of $\MPP$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-105: The rest of this section is devoted to monotone path polytopes of hypersimplices, and especially hypersimplices for $k = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Before presenting new results on this subject, we shortly recall two former results from Billera and Sturmfels \cite[end of Section 5]{BilleraSturmfels-FiberPolytope .}
elements.sent-2411-107: These two results motivate the study of the monotone path polytopes of hypersimplices.
elements.sent-2411-108: Indeed, hypersimplices are a generalization of simplices, and arise as sections of the standard cube.
elements.sent-2411-109: For $n\geq 2$, $k\in [n]$, the \defn{$(n,k)$-hypersimplex is $\HypSimpl = \bigl\{\b x\in[0,1]^n ~;~ \sum_i x_i = k\bigr\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-110: It is the section of the standard cube $\Cube = [0,1]^n$ by the hyperplane $\left\{\b x\in \R^n ~;~\inner{\b x, (1, \dots, 1) = k\right\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The vertices of $\HypSimpl$ are exactly its $(0,1)$-coordinate elements: the $(0,1)$-vectors with $k$ ones and $n-k$ zeros.
elements.sent-2411-112: We denote the \defn{support of a vertex $\b v\in V(\HypSimpl)$ by $\supp := \{i ~;~ v_i = 1\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Two vertices $\b u,\b v\in V(\HypSimpl)$ share an edge when $|\supp[\b u]\cap\supp| = k-1$, \ie to obtain $\b v$ from $\b u$, flip a zero to a one and a one to a zero.
elements.sent-2411-114: Note that the hypersimplices $\HypSimpl[n][1]$ and $\HypSimpl[n][n-1]$ are simplices: in this sense, hypersimplices are a generalization of simplices.
elements.sent-2411-115: As sections of the standard cube, they also generalize the cube.
elements.sent-2411-116: We consider the linear program $(\HypSimpl,\b c)$ where $\b c\in \R^n$ is generic with respect to $\HypSimpl$.
elements.sent-2411-117: A direction $\b c$ is generic for the hypersimplex when $c_i\ne c_j$ for all $i\ne j$, as each edge has direction $\b e_i - \b e_j$.
elements.sent-2411-118: Without loss of generality, as the hypersimplex is invariant under reordering coordinates, we suppose $c_1 < c_2 < \dots < c_n$.

elements.sent-2411-433: Computing monotone path polytopes is the entry door to the larger subject of fiber polytopes, for which explicitly computing examples remains a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-434: We first give a detailed presentation on how to construct monotone path polytopes.
elements.sent-2411-435: Monotone path polytopes of cubes and simplices have been known since the seminal article of Billera and Sturmfels \cite{BilleraSturmfels-FiberPolytope .}
elements.sent-2411-436: We extend these results to hypersimplices by linking this problem to the combinatorics of lattice paths.
elements.sent-2411-437: Indeed, we give a combinatorial model which describes the vertices of the monotone path polytope of the hypersimplex $\HypSimplTwo$ (for any generic direction).

elements.sent-2411-439: After their introduction by Billera and Sturmfels \cite{BilleraSturmfels-FiberPolytope , fiber polytopes received a lot of attention.}
elements.sent-2411-440: In particular, the fiber polytope for the projection of a polytope $\polytopeP$ onto a segment encapsulates the combinatorics of (coherent) monotone paths on $\polytopeP$: it is the \defn{monotone path polytope of $\polytopeP$ \cite{athanasiadis1999piles,athanasiadis2000monotone,blanchard2020length .}}
elements.sent-2411-441: Its vertices are in bijection with the monotone paths that can be followed by a shadow vertex rule.
elements.sent-2411-442: Recently, Olarte and Santos \cite{OlarteSantos-HypersimplicialSubdivisions introduced $k^\text{th$-hypersecondary polytopes which is fiber polytope for the projection of the hypersimplex $\HypSimpl$ onto (the $k^\text{th}$ Minkowski sum of) a point configuration.}}
elements.sent-2411-443: Although not discussed in \cite{OlarteSantos-HypersimplicialSubdivisions , when this point configuration is contained into a line, then the hypersecondary polytope is equal to the monotone path polytope of the hypersimplex $\HypSimpl$.}
